paragraphs.body: “No matter where. No matter what.” This saying holds a special place in my sister’s and I heart. We loved this saying based on the fact that no matter where or what is going on inside our lives, through the good and the bad, and to the sun and moon back, we will always have each other backs. When I first read the topic of this assignment, I had no idea what my home language was going to be at first, but after a long time thinking about it, the language finally came to mind. To me, a home language does not have to be a spoken or written language, but rather a more permanent language. Tattoos are the spoken language I speak with my mom, sister, and aunt. It is a language that is not just a form of art but holds great significance to that specific person. Many outsiders cannot understand the logic of getting something permanently drawn onto your body.

annotations.text: Tattoos are the spoken language I speak with my mom, sister, and aunt. It is a language that is not just a form of art but holds great significance to that specific person.

paragraphs.body: The memory of me getting my first tattoo will forever hold a special place in my heart. My mom, aunt, and I went up to Slippery Rock University to meet my sister who attended there. We spent months planning out this day, to give each of us time to think about what tattoo we were going to get and the placement of them. When deciding on what I wanted my first tattoo to be, I wanted to get something that showed what I had overcome in my life. I chose to get two small butterflies on the left side of my ribs that have a semicolon and the NEDA symbol as the body. The semicolon represents mental health awareness, resilience, and the strength to keep going. The NEDA symbol represents a reminder of hope, strength, and recovery for those who were affected by an eating disorder. These two symbols show that even though I suffered from two very hard things in my life, I choose strength to become better and wanted to show awareness to anyone else who suffers from them that it is possible to overcome it. My aunt picked out a tattoo that said mind over matter with an arrow representing the over on her forearm. She chose to get this tattoo to symbolize for all purposes in life: you can set your mind to with an arrow pointing away from her to say always look ahead, not behind. My mom got a fancy heart tattoo on her forearm because she simply just likes hearts. My sister got a small cross tattoo on her quad to express her faith and protection. Not only did getting a tattoo that meant a great deal to me hold a special place in my heart, but it was also the experience of getting my first tattoo with the people I love and care about. During the whole process of each of us getting our tattoos created a powerful bond between all of us. We were jamming out to whatever music the tattoo artist was playing, having the most random conversations, and laughing constantly at any jokes someone made. This experience created a safe space where vulnerability is welcomed and shared memories serve as a reminder of the bonds that hold us together.

annotations.text: This experience created a safe space where vulnerability is welcomed and shared memories serve as a reminder of the bonds that hold us together.
annotations.comment: I'm interested in this line! I wonder if you have experiences where vulnerability is _not_ welcomed? What does it mean to be vulnerable?

annotations.context_left: The memory of me getting my first tattoo will forever hold a special place in my heart.

paragraphs.body: My sister and I had always talked about getting matching tattoos when we were older. We wanted something to represent the difference between us, but also something that had a special meaning. We spent months showing each other different matching tattoos on Pinterest, but we could never agree on one. It was not until I came across this one tattoo that said, “No matter where, no matter what,” that had a sun and moon at the end of one the two saying. Instantly, we both loved this saying because it symbolized how our relationship is truly. To us, the sun and moon highlight our different personalities but the unbreakable bond that we share with each other. The two sayings symbolize the unwavering support, the unconditional love we have for each other, and that no matter what challenges faced, we will always be there for each other no matter where we are in the world. I got the saying, “no matter where,” with sun at the end, and my sister got the saying, “no matter what,” with the moon at the end. We decided to place these tattoos in the same spot on our upper thigh, so when we put them together it will read out the saying. When we got these tattoos, I recall I made the comment to the tattoo artist, “Wait you’re already done?” My sister looked at me like I was crazy then started bursting out laughing because she never expected that to come out of my mouth. My sister is the only person present when I got all five of my tattoos. She is one of the only people I love sharing this experience with. Tattoos helped create a strong bond between my sister and I that signifies the hardships we each encounter through our lives.

annotations.text: Tattoos helped create a strong bond between my sister and I that signifies the hardships we each encounter through our lives.
annotations.comment: I wonder if this bond has anything to do with vulnerability? I'm not sure, but I'd like to talk about it!

paragraphs.body: My dad, grandparents, brother, friends, and outsiders do not understand the concept of getting something permanently inked onto your body for the rest of your life. They think tattoos are just silly pieces of art that represent no significant meaning at all. In some cases, they are correct. Sometimes people just get tattoos because they look “cool”, or they are “fashionable.” Other times people get tattoos to showcase their personalities, the hardships they overcome, motivation, or in memory of a loved one. I can recall at times when I would go visit my grandma and grandfather, or when my dad noticed a new tattoo my sister and I got. They would always make comments to us on why we waste our money on these things. They think we can use that money on more important things than a simple permanent piece of ink. Outsiders believe that tattoos are a form a rebellion, unprofessional or inappropriate, a fashion statement rather than meaningful expressions, or views them as negativity wrong. But for me, these tattoos hold great meaning to who I am as a person. To me, they are not just fragments of art or random sayings. Each one of my tattoos has a significant meaning to them. I always have to think long and hard before I commit to getting something permanently tattooed onto my body. My tattoos represent the hardships of mental health I overcame in my life, the special bond I have with my sister, the important saying that I am enough, the truth that I am a survivor of a deadly disease, and the memory of my grandfather who died.

annotations.text: My dad, grandparents, brother, friends, and outsiders do not understand the concept of getting something permanently inked onto your body for the rest of your life.

paragraphs.body: To speak in tattoos is not just a form of art, It gives us the ability to freely voice concerns without being dismissed, It symbolizes my reclaiming of my voice, It helps me remember that my needs and opinions have equal value, It holds meaning or significance, Whether it is small, discreet tattoos or a full sleeve, Tattoos can be a beautiful and meaningful addition. So next time you see someone with tattoos, do not automatically assume that they permanently put that onto their body to look cool. Maybe ask them what the meanings or significance of them is. Maybe sit back and think that tattoos hold more than just a little message.
